title: Bloody diarrhea in an older anticoagulated man after recent antibiotics

chart.oneLiner: 68M with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, atrial fibrillation on apixaban, and CKD stage 3 presents with 3 days of frequent bloody diarrhea, crampy abdominal pain, fever, and lightheadedness, two weeks after a course of amoxicillin-clavulanate for sinusitis and a few days after a backyard barbecue.

opening: Doc, I've had terrible diarrhea for three days, there's blood in it now, and I feel dizzy when I stand up.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 68-year-old man, tired and mildly dehydrated, a little lightheaded. Cooperative and a reasonable historian, but tends to under-report. Worried because he is on a blood thinner and is now seeing blood.

HPI: 3 days of frequent loose stools (8-12x/day), crampy lower abdominal pain, and low-grade fevers. For the last day the stool has had visible blood and mucus mixed in. Lightheaded and weak when standing; has not been able to keep up with fluids; urinating less than usual. Mild nausea, no real vomiting.
EXPOSURES (reveal only when asked about food, travel, or sick contacts): ate chicken and potato salad at a backyard barbecue ~4 days before onset; thinks the chicken may have been pink in the middle. Two other people at the barbecue also got diarrhea. NO travel abroad. NO sick contacts at home.
ANTIBIOTIC / HEALTHCARE EXPOSURE (reveal only when asked about recent antibiotics, recent illness, or hospital/clinic visits): finished a 7-day course of amoxicillin-clavulanate about 2 weeks ago for a sinus infection. NO hospitalization. NO nursing-home stay. NO recent colonoscopy.
BLEEDING / ANTICOAGULATION (reveal only when asked about blood thinners or bleeding): takes apixaban for atrial fibrillation, took it as scheduled this morning; the blood appears mixed through the loose stool rather than as separate bright-red clots, no black tarry stools, no vomiting blood, no nosebleeds or gum bleeding.
PMH: type 2 diabetes (~10 years), hypertension, atrial fibrillation, chronic kidney disease (told his kidneys are 'about half'). Baseline creatinine around 1.4. NO known inflammatory bowel disease, NO prior similar episodes.
MEDS (reveal on direct medication questioning): apixaban; lisinopril; hydrochlorothiazide; metformin; atorvastatin; recently finished amoxicillin-clavulanate. He has continued all of them including the metformin, lisinopril, and water pill despite the diarrhea. ALLERGIES: none.
SOCIAL: retired teacher, lives with wife, social alcohol only, never smoker, no drugs, no recent travel.
FAMILY: noncontributory.
ROS: as above; reduced urine output, no chest pain, no palpitations he notices, no leg swelling, no rash, no joint pains, no mouth ulcers, no weight loss before this illness.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: tired, mildly dehydrated, dry mucous membranes.
- Vitals: BP 104/64 (orthostatic: drops to 88/58 with HR rise to 124 on standing), HR 108, RR 18, 38.5C, SpO2 97% RA.
- Abdomen: diffuse mild-to-moderate tenderness, worst in the lower abdomen, active/hyperactive bowel sounds, NO rigidity, NO rebound, NO localized peritonism.
- Cardiac: irregularly irregular rhythm, no murmur. Lungs clear. No focal neuro deficit. No peripheral edema.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the undercooked chicken, sick contacts, recent antibiotics, or the apixaban/bleeding details unless asked about the relevant topic (food/exposures, sick contacts, recent antibiotics, or blood thinners/bleeding).
- Reveal exam findings only when the relevant maneuver is performed (e.g. orthostatic vitals only if orthostatics are taken).
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.StoolStudies: Fecal leukocytes PRESENT (inflammatory diarrhea); stool culture and multiplex GI pathogen panel sent; C. difficile NAAT/toxin sent separately. (Culture/panel later: Campylobacter jejuni; STEC/Shiga-toxin assay negative.)
labs.Cdiff: C. difficile toxin EIA negative and NAAT negative — argues against C. difficile colitis despite the recent amoxicillin-clavulanate.
labs.CBC: WBC 14.8 with left shift, Hgb 13.8 (near baseline — argues against major GI blood loss), Plt 240.
labs.BMP: Na 134, K 5.8, Cl 102, HCO3 16, BUN 52, Cr 2.4 (baseline ~1.4 -> acute kidney injury on CKD); glucose 168. Anion gap = Na - (Cl + HCO3) = 134 - (102 + 16) = 16 (elevated). Hyponatremia (thiazide + volume loss), hyperkalemia (AKI + ACE inhibitor), and a high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis are all present concurrently.
labs.Lactate: Lactate 3.6 mmol/L (elevated) — from hypovolemia/hypoperfusion, compounded by continued metformin in the setting of AKI; helps explain the elevated anion gap.
labs.Coags: INR 1.2 (apixaban is not reflected by INR); no laboratory coagulopathy to explain bloody stool — the blood is inflammatory/dysenteric, not a primary bleeding problem.
labs.BloodCultures: Drawn given fever and tachycardia; pending.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Tall, peaked, narrow-based symmetric T waves (best seen in the precordial leads) with the underlying atrial fibrillation; early hyperkalemia pattern at K 5.8 with AKI on an ACE inhibitor.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: tall peaked symmetric T waves of hyperkalemia in a patient in atrial fibrillation. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead in atrial fibrillation with tall, peaked, narrow-based symmetric T waves and a narrow QRS — early hyperkalemia (K 5.8).
searchTerms: tall tented T waves potassium ECG
ekg.clinicalVignette: An older man with diarrheal volume depletion, acute kidney injury, and an ACE inhibitor has tall, peaked, narrow-based T waves on his tracing.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Left lower lobe volume loss with a linear opacity and slight mediastinal shift toward the opacity.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Left lower lobe volume loss with a linear opacity and slight mediastinal shift toward the opacity. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph showing atelectasis volume loss
cxr.clinicalVignette: A 62-year-old woman presents with fever, cough, and mild dyspnea after a recent gastrointestinal illness.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for this older man's bloody diarrhea (note your leading diagnosis). Explicitly name three distinct etiologies/mechanisms of bloody (inflammatory) diarrhea you are considering and how this patient's recent antibiotics, anticoagulation, and vascular comorbidities change the differential.
steps.rubric: Bacterial (inflammatory/dysenteric) gastroenteritis — Campylobacter/Salmonella/Shigella from undercooked poultry (leading,5). C. difficile colitis — must consider given amoxicillin-clavulanate 2 weeks ago (CANT-MISS,4). Shiga-toxin E. coli / STEC — must-not-miss because it changes management and risks HUS (CANT-MISS,3). Ischemic colitis — must consider in an older vasculopath with AF/diabetes and hypotension (CANT-MISS,3). Inflammatory bowel disease, new onset (core,2). Anticoagulant-related GI bleed as a confounder to exclude (core,2). Names THREE distinct mechanisms of bloody diarrhea — e.g. invasive bacterial mucosal infection, toxin-mediated colitis (C. difficile or STEC), and mucosal ischemia/inflammation (core,4). PENALTY: anchors on simple food poisoning and ignores recent antibiotics, anticoagulation, or ischemia (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: bacterial (inflammatory/dysenteric) gastroenteritis — Campylobacter/Salmonella/Shigella after undercooked barbecue chicken with sick contacts. Must-not-miss/competing: C. difficile colitis (he finished amoxicillin-clavulanate ~2 weeks ago), Shiga-toxin E. coli/STEC (HUS risk, changes management), and ischemic colitis (older diabetic/AF vasculopath who is now hypotensive). Also reasonable: new inflammatory bowel disease, and an anticoagulant-related GI bleed to exclude as a confounder for the visible blood. Three distinct mechanisms of bloody diarrhea: (1) invasive bacterial mucosal infection, (2) toxin-mediated colitis (C. difficile toxin or Shiga toxin), and (3) mucosal ischemia/inflammation (ischemic colitis or IBD).

criticalActions.item: Ischemic colitis in an older vasculopath

coreActions.item: Names three distinct mechanisms of bloody diarrhea (invasive infection, toxin-mediated colitis, mucosal ischemia/inflammation)

penalties.item: Anchors on simple food poisoning and ignores recent antibiotics, anticoagulation, or ischemia

mustNotMiss: Ischemic colitis in an older vasculopath

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Stool studies — culture and/or multiplex GI pathogen panel including Shiga-toxin assay (CANT-MISS,4). C. difficile toxin/NAAT given recent antibiotics (CANT-MISS,3). BMP for AKI/electrolytes — specifically potassium and bicarbonate (CANT-MISS,3). CBC (1). Lactate given hypotension/possible ischemia and metformin (1). EKG to look for hyperkalemia changes (1). CXR (1). Type & screen / coags given anticoagulation and bloody stool (bonus,1). PENALTY: empiric antibiotics before STEC is excluded, or no potassium/renal check in an anticoagulated diabetic on an ACE inhibitor (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: Stool studies (culture and multiplex GI panel WITH a Shiga-toxin/STEC assay); C. difficile toxin/NAAT (recent amoxicillin-clavulanate); BMP focused on potassium, bicarbonate, and renal function (AKI on an ACE inhibitor + thiazide); CBC; Lactate (hypotension, possible ischemia, continued metformin); EKG to look for hyperkalemia; CXR; type & screen / coags given anticoagulation and visible blood. Do NOT give empiric antibiotics before STEC is excluded.

criticalActions.item: Stool studies — culture/GI panel including Shiga-toxin assay
C. difficile toxin/NAAT given recent antibiotics
BMP for AKI and electrolytes (potassium, bicarbonate)

coreActions.item: EKG to look for hyperkalemia changes

bonusActions.item: Type & screen / coags given anticoagulation and bloody stool

penalties.item: Empiric antibiotics before STEC excluded, or no potassium/renal check in an anticoagulated diabetic on an ACE inhibitor

requiredTests: Stool studies — culture/GI panel including Shiga-toxin assay
C. difficile toxin/NAAT given recent antibiotics
BMP for AKI and electrolytes (potassium, bicarbonate)

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is THIS patient's 12-lead EKG, taken in the department. Describe your findings and their significance given his labs.
steps.rubric: Findings = tall, peaked, narrow-based symmetric T waves (precordial leads) on a background of atrial fibrillation — early hyperkalemia (K 5.8) from AKI on an ACE inhibitor with volume depletion. CREDIT: identifies peaked/tented T waves (6); attributes them to hyperkalemia and links to the K 5.8 / AKI rather than ischemia (5); notes the underlying atrial fibrillation / systematic read (2). PENALTY: dismisses the peaked T waves as normal or calls them hyperacute ischemic T waves and misses the metabolic cause (-3).
keyFindings: attributes to hyperkalemia (K 5.8) from AKI on an ACE inhibitor, not ischemia
criticalFindings: Peaked T waves of hyperkalemia (K 5.8) in atrial fibrillation — an electrically active, treatable emergency.
commonMistakes: dismissing the peaked T waves as normal
calling them hyperacute ischemic T waves and missing the hyperkalemia
steps.idealAnswer: Tall, peaked, narrow-based, symmetric T waves (best seen in the precordial leads) on a background of atrial fibrillation. This is the early electrocardiographic signature of hyperkalemia, fitting his K of 5.8 from acute kidney injury on an ACE inhibitor and thiazide with diarrheal volume loss — NOT a primary ischemic event. It flags the hyperkalemia as an electrically active, immediately treatable emergency.

criticalActions: Peaked T waves of hyperkalemia (K 5.8) in atrial fibrillation — an electrically active, treatable emergency.
coreActions: attributes to hyperkalemia (K 5.8) from AKI on an ACE inhibitor, not ischemia

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the hyperkalemia / mislabels as ischemia

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is a chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = atelectasis: left lower lobe volume loss with a linear/band opacity and slight ipsilateral mediastinal shift toward the opacity. CREDIT: identifies the linear/band opacity with volume loss (6); distinguishes atelectasis from consolidation/pneumonia (4). PENALTY: calling the film normal or labelling it pneumonia (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Atelectasis: left basal linear opacity with volume loss and ipsilateral mediastinal shift.

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: The stool panel shows inflammatory diarrhea with fecal leukocytes and Campylobacter; the C. difficile NAAT/toxin is negative and the Shiga-toxin assay is negative. The EKG shows peaked T waves and the BMP shows K 5.8, an anion gap of 16, and AKI on CKD. Commit to your leading diagnosis, name the discriminating tests that resolved the differential, and state the residual ambiguity you would keep watching.
steps.rubric: Bacterial gastroenteritis (Campylobacter from undercooked poultry) confirmed by fecal leukocytes + Campylobacter on culture/panel (6). Names the discriminators that resolved the competing diagnoses — negative C. difficile NAAT/toxin makes C. difficile colitis unlikely despite recent antibiotics; negative Shiga-toxin assay makes STEC unlikely and permits antibiotics if needed; Hgb at baseline and INR 1.2 argue against an anticoagulant GI bleed; and identifies the CONCURRENT problems to manage in parallel — hyperkalemia (peaked T waves, K 5.8), AKI on CKD, and an anion-gap acidosis (lactate 3.6, metformin) (4). Acknowledges residual uncertainty — ischemic colitis can coexist, so monitor and consider endoscopy if it does not improve.
steps.idealAnswer: Commit to bacterial gastroenteritis, most likely Campylobacter from undercooked barbecue chicken — supported by fecal leukocytes (inflammatory diarrhea) and Campylobacter on the stool panel. Discriminating tests resolved the fork: the negative C. difficile NAAT/toxin makes C. difficile colitis unlikely despite the recent amoxicillin-clavulanate; the negative Shiga-toxin assay makes STEC unlikely and means antibiotics are not contraindicated on HUS grounds; Hgb at baseline with INR 1.2 argues against an apixaban-related GI bleed (the blood is dysenteric). At the same time, recognize the concurrent, separate problems to treat in parallel: hyperkalemia (peaked T waves, K 5.8) from AKI on an ACE inhibitor, the AKI on CKD from volume depletion, and a high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis (gap 16; lactate 3.6, worsened by continued metformin). Residual ambiguity: ischemic colitis remains possible in this hypotensive vasculopath, so monitor closely and pursue endoscopy/CT if he fails to improve.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan, including how you handle his electrolytes, his home medications, and his disposition (is he safe for discharge)?
steps.rubric: Per MGH diarrhea + hyperkalemia: aggressive IV volume and electrolyte repletion for hypotension/AKI (CANT-MISS,5); TREAT the hyperkalemia — calcium to stabilize the myocardium plus insulin/dextrose +/- shift agents, recheck K and EKG (CANT-MISS,5); HOLD the nephrotoxic/contributing home meds — lisinopril, hydrochlorothiazide, and metformin during AKI (CANT-MISS,3); selective directed antibiotic (azithromycin) now that STEC is excluded and given severe/bloody disease in an older host (3); AVOID antimotility agents in febrile bloody diarrhea (2); address anticoagulation — hold/withhold apixaban given active GI bleeding and AKI, weigh AF stroke risk (2); admit rather than discharge — hemodynamically significant illness with AKI and hyperkalemia is NOT safe for discharge; arrange admission with med reconciliation, repeat labs, and clear return/follow-up criteria for the eventual safe discharge (3). PENALTY: discharging this hypotensive patient with hyperkalemia/AKI (-4); giving empiric antibiotics before STEC excluded or antimotility in febrile bloody diarrhea (-3); continuing the ACE inhibitor/metformin during AKI (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Resuscitate first: IV isotonic fluids for hypotension, orthostasis, and AKI, with electrolyte repletion. Treat the hyperkalemia as an emergency given the peaked T waves — IV calcium to stabilize the myocardium, then insulin with dextrose (and other shift/elimination agents as needed), and recheck the potassium and EKG. Hold the contributing home medications during the AKI: stop lisinopril and hydrochlorothiazide (worsening AKI/hyperkalemia/hyponatremia) and hold metformin (lactic-acidosis risk with AKI). Because the Shiga-toxin assay is negative and this is severe, bloody, febrile diarrhea in an older host, a directed antibiotic (azithromycin for Campylobacter) is reasonable; AVOID antimotility agents like loperamide in febrile bloody diarrhea. Address anticoagulation: withhold apixaban while there is active GI bleeding and AKI, balancing his atrial-fibrillation stroke risk, and reassess. Disposition: he is NOT safe for discharge — he is hypotensive with AKI and hyperkalemia; admit for monitoring, repeat labs/EKG, and IV therapy. Plan the eventual safe discharge with medication reconciliation (when to restart the ACE inhibitor/diuretic/metformin), and clear return precautions and follow-up.

criticalActions.item: Aggressive IV volume and electrolyte repletion for hypotension/AKI
Treat hyperkalemia: calcium + insulin/dextrose, recheck K and EKG
Hold nephrotoxic/contributing meds (lisinopril, hydrochlorothiazide, metformin) during AKI

coreActions.item: Directed antibiotic (azithromycin) now that STEC is excluded, severe bloody disease in older host
Avoid antimotility agents in febrile bloody diarrhea
Hold/withhold apixaban for active GI bleeding and AKI, weigh AF stroke risk
Admit (not safe for discharge); plan safe discharge with med reconciliation and return/follow-up criteria

bonusActions.item: Oral rehydration / dietary advice once stable

penalties.item: Discharging this hypotensive patient with hyperkalemia/AKI
Empiric antibiotics before STEC excluded or antimotility in febrile bloody diarrhea

requiredActions: Aggressive IV volume and electrolyte repletion for hypotension/AKI
Treat hyperkalemia: calcium + insulin/dextrose, recheck K and EKG
Hold nephrotoxic/contributing meds (lisinopril, hydrochlorothiazide, metformin) during AKI
optionalActions: Directed antibiotic (azithromycin) now that STEC is excluded, severe bloody disease in older host
Avoid antimotility agents in febrile bloody diarrhea
Hold/withhold apixaban for active GI bleeding and AKI, weigh AF stroke risk
Admit (not safe for discharge); plan safe discharge with med reconciliation and return/follow-up criteria
Oral rehydration / dietary advice once stable
unsafeActions: Discharging this hypotensive patient with hyperkalemia/AKI
Empiric antibiotics before STEC excluded or antimotility in febrile bloody diarrhea
steps.disposition: Admit (hypotension, AKI, hyperkalemia); plan safe discharge after stabilization and med reconciliation

physicalExamMappings.finding: General: tired, mildly dehydrated (dry mucous membranes).
Orthostatic vitals positive: BP drops from 104/64 to 88/58 with HR rising to 124 on standing — significant volume depletion.
Abdomen: diffuse mild-to-moderate tenderness, worst in the lower abdomen; NO rigidity, NO rebound, NO localized peritonism.
Cardiac: irregularly irregular rhythm, no murmur (known atrial fibrillation).
Dry mucous membranes consistent with dehydration.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 68M with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, atrial fibrillation on apixaban, and CKD stage 3 presents with 3 days of bloody inflammatory diarrhea, fever, and orthostatic hypotension after undercooked barbecue chicken, two weeks after amoxicillin-clavulanate. The diagnosis is Bacterial gastroenteritis (Campylobacter), reached by working a genuine fork against C. difficile colitis, STEC, and ischemic colitis, and by recognizing concurrent multi-system derangements — hyperkalemia (peaked T waves on his own EKG), AKI on CKD, and a high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis — driven by his comorbidity and polypharmacy.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: General: tired, mildly dehydrated (dry mucous membranes).
Orthostatic vitals positive: BP drops from 104/64 to 88/58 with HR rising to 124 on standing — significant volume depletion.
Abdomen: diffuse mild-to-moderate tenderness, worst in the lower abdomen; NO rigidity, NO rebound, NO localized peritonism.
Cardiac: irregularly irregular rhythm, no murmur (known atrial fibrillation).
Dry mucous membranes consistent with dehydration.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals, noting the hypotension and tachycardia.
Elicit the focused history, drawing out the undercooked-poultry exposure, sick contacts, recent amoxicillin-clavulanate, and the apixaban/bleeding details only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted exam, including orthostatic vitals, to reveal volume depletion and a non-peritonitic abdomen.
Build a differential led by bacterial gastroenteritis while explicitly forking against C. difficile colitis, STEC, and ischemic colitis and naming three mechanisms of bloody diarrhea.
Order stool studies with a Shiga-toxin assay, C. difficile testing, and a BMP; read the patient's own EKG as hyperkalemia and integrate the concurrent AKI and anion-gap acidosis.

Commit to bacterial gastroenteritis, treat the hyperkalemia and volume depletion in parallel, hold the contributing home meds, and admit rather than discharge — planning a later safe discharge with med reconciliation.
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on simple food poisoning and ignoring recent antibiotics, anticoagulation, or ischemia
Missing the hyperkalemia on the EKG or mislabeling the peaked T waves as ischemia
Giving empiric antibiotics before STEC is excluded, or antimotility agents in febrile bloody diarrhea
Continuing the ACE inhibitor and metformin during AKI
Discharging a hypotensive patient with AKI and hyperkalemia
managementPearls: Treat hyperkalemia as an emergency: calcium then insulin/dextrose, recheck K and EKG
Resuscitate volume and AKI while holding the ACE inhibitor, thiazide, and metformin
criticalWorkupItems: Stool studies — culture/GI panel including Shiga-toxin assay
C. difficile toxin/NAAT given recent antibiotics
BMP for AKI and electrolytes (potassium, bicarbonate)
criticalInterpretationItems: Peaked T waves of hyperkalemia (K 5.8) in atrial fibrillation — an electrically active, treatable emergency.
criticalManagementItems: Aggressive IV volume and electrolyte repletion for hypotension/AKI
Treat hyperkalemia: calcium + insulin/dextrose, recheck K and EKG
Hold nephrotoxic/contributing meds (lisinopril, hydrochlorothiazide, metformin) during AKI
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Peaked T waves of hyperkalemia (K 5.8) in atrial fibrillation — an electrically active, treatable emergency.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Peaked T waves of hyperkalemia (K 5.8) in atrial fibrillation — an electrically active, treatable emergency.
imageInterpretationPearls: Peaked, narrow-based T waves in a patient with AKI on an ACE inhibitor are hyperkalemia until proven otherwise — read the EKG against the chemistry.
An integrated tracing is part of the puzzle: name the finding, then tie it to the metabolic cause and the treatment.
Read every study systematically and state pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Elicits the patient's concerns / ICE (worry about bleeding on a blood thinner)
Asks permission before sensitive medication/bleeding questions

libraryUpgrade.imagePolicy: integrated-read where clinically natural; pathology-forward standalone otherwise
